title: Rahm's Message: Six-Shot Mexico City Masterclass
excerpt: Jon Rahm delivered a dominant display at LIV Golf Mexico City, winning by six shots and claiming his 16th trophy — the most of any player in LIV history.
body:
Rahm Takes Control in Mexico City

Jon Rahm's message to the rest of LIV Golf was unmistakable on Sunday at Club de Golf Chapultepec: I am not to be caught. The Legion XIII captain orchestrated a bogey-free 7-under 64 in the final round, producing the week's lowest score and a commanding six-shot victory over young David Puig of Fireballs GC. It was Rahm's most decisive win since joining LIV prior to the 2024 season and marked his third consecutive Individual Championship heading into Mexico City — a streak that feels less like fortune and more like inevitability.

Winning Score
-21
Victory Margin
6 Shots
Rahm LIV Trophies
16
Legion XIII Team Win
9 Shots

The Decisive Four Holes

The tournament was effectively decided in holes two through five. After opening with a par, Rahm drove the 318-yard par-4 second green, two-putted from 66 feet for birdie, then drove the 390-yard third and tapped in from 3 feet for eagle. Birdies on the next two holes extended his lead to an insurmountable six shots before the turn. Tyrrell Hatton, his Legion XIII teammate and overnight second, doubled the drivable second hole and never recovered.

Spanish Dominance

Rahm's victory completed a Spanish sweep of the podium — a moment of genuine national pride. David Puig, the 24-year-old Fireballs star who finished runner-up, described the bond between the three Spaniards atop the leaderboard as virtually inseparable. "I spend more time with [Puig] than I do with my wife or kids," Rahm said candidly. Josele Ballester, 22 and in third place, rounded out an extraordinary showcase of Spanish golf talent, with Puig and Ballester representing the present and future of the generation learning at Rahm's feet.

The Record Book

With his Mexico City sweep — individual title plus team victory — Rahm claimed his 16th LIV Golf trophy, surpassing Bryson DeChambeau's previous high of 15. The Spaniard has done it in two fewer seasons: 4 tournament titles, 2 Individual Championships, 9 team tournament titles, and 1 Team Championship. He is now the winningest player in league history, despite having played just six tournaments this season. At 21 under, he finished two shots better than his opening score, a testament to his adaptive brilliance and relentless appetite.

What's Next

Rahm is marching toward a third consecutive Individual Championship with two wins, three solo seconds, and a solo fifth in just six starts — a pace that would represent one of the most dominant stretches in LIV history. The next tournament comes to Virginia on May 7–10. For the chasing pack, it's becoming increasingly clear: stop Jon Rahm, or simply watch him add to his historic tally.
